result:
I’ve been thinking about how we treat time like a ledger—hours owed, minutes spent, interest compounded in sleepless nights. We speak of “killing” it, “saving” it, as if it were a stubborn insect or a coupon. But time isn’t currency; it’s weather. It rolls over us whether we barcode it or not, warm fronts of laughter, sudden hail of diagnosis. You can’t store a thunderstorm in a savings account.  

Yesterday I watched a child on the subway offer a crumpled sticker to a tired stranger. The paper heart landed on a business card, migrated to a leather briefcase, and rode away. Nothing measurable changed: GDP stayed put, the train remained late. Yet the air in that car shifted, a micro-climate of shared oxygen. I counted twenty-three shoulders softening.  

We keep waiting for grand circuses of meaning—weddings, diplomas, asteroid touchdowns—while tiny trapeze acts swing above our eyelashes. A neighbor hums off-key at 7:04 a.m.; the cat chooses your lap although you never fed it; someone repairs the cracked sidewalk before you trip. No app logs these rescues, but they accumulate like candle smoke on a ceiling, invisible architecture holding the house together.  

So I’m deleting the productivity spreadsheet where I was courting future applause. Instead I’m adopting an imaginary dog named Hour. No leash, no bowl. We just walk, sniffing the wind for approaching wonder, chasing sticks thrown by no one. When friends ask what I’m doing with my life, I shrug: “Training Hour to sit, stay, roll over into the next astonishment.”
